1:
“Now when Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judaea in the days of Herod the king, behold, there came wise men from the east to Jerusalem.” The witnesses note how the Evangelist fixes the place, the time, and the strange visitors. The grammatical-historical commentators mark “Bethlehem of Judaea” — distinguished, Jerome and the Gloss note, from another Bethlehem in Zebulun — the town foretold for Messiah’s birth; and “the days of Herod the king,” the Idumaean whose death Ellicott dates to 4 BC. The Latin Rabanus and Wesley find in the foreign king the fulfilment of Jacob’s word: “the sceptre shall not depart from Judah… until Shiloh come” (Genesis 49:10) — the rule having passed to a stranger, the Messiah was due. The “wise men” (Magi) were, the commentators agree, learned men of the East, students of the heavens; Chrysostom and Gregory guard against the Priscillianist abuse of the star to support astrology, the new star being no fate but a sign.

The fathers dwell on the wonder that Gentiles were the first to seek the new-born King. Pseudo-Augustine asks why the Magi sought out this King of Judaea when they had sought none before, and answers that this one alone “spoke from heaven” by his star. Leo sees in them the firstfruits of “that innumerable posterity once promised to Abraham,” called “not after the flesh, but by the Spirit.” The Eastern Pseudo-Chrysostom connects the time to the prophecy of Daniel’s seventy weeks. Henry, for the Puritan expositors, marks the humiliation of Christ’s obscure birth, “little observed,” save by these strangers. The witnesses thus find in the verse the meeting of prophecy and grace. The King is born exactly where and when the Scriptures foretold — in Bethlehem, in the last days of Judah’s independence — and the first to honour him are not the priests of Jerusalem but wise men from the Gentile East, led by a star. The coming of the Magi, the witnesses note, is the dawn of the gospel’s reach to the nations: the Saviour of the Jews is sought and worshipped, from the first, by the heathen, a sign that he is the desire of all nations and the light of the Gentiles.
2:
“Saying, Where is he that is born King of the Jews? for we have seen his star in the east, and are come to worship him.” The witnesses dwell on the Magi’s question, the star, and their purpose. The grammatical-historical commentators note the wide expectation of the age: Ellicott observes that the Roman historians Tacitus and Suetonius record a belief “very widely diffused” that a ruler should arise from Judaea. Robertson clarifies the star “in the east” — better, “at its rising” (en tē anatolē) — for they did not follow a star eastward but came west; they had seen its rising in their own land. Lightfoot takes it of a special “heavenly light” that shone the night of the Saviour’s birth, not an ordinary star. Many connect it with Balaam’s prophecy, “there shall come a Star out of Jacob” (Numbers 24:17), known in the East.

The witnesses dwell on the Magi’s aim: “to worship him.” Wesley reads it of the homage “the eastern nations used to pay to their monarchs,” the bowing to the earth before a king; yet the witnesses find in it more — the seeking of the new-born King not to flatter but to adore. The Eastern Chrysostom harmonises their search with Luke’s manger, the holy family having found no house. The witnesses thus find in the verse the faith of the Gentile seekers set against the indifference of Jerusalem. Strangers from afar, guided only by a star and the rumour of prophecy, ask boldly in the capital, “Where is he that is born King of the Jews?” — taking his birth for certain and his royalty for granted — while the city that held the oracles of God had not stirred to seek him. Their long journey, their confident question, and their declared purpose to worship rebuke the apathy of those near at hand. The witnesses note the irony that runs through the chapter: the Gentiles seek the King of the Jews to adore him, while his own king seeks him to destroy him, and his own people, though they know the prophecies, do not so much as go to see.
3:
“When Herod the king had heard these things, he was troubled, and all Jerusalem with him.” The witnesses note the guilty fear of the tyrant and the strange dismay of the city. The grammatical-historical commentators explain Herod’s alarm: having “obtained the kingdom by great crimes and much blood” (Barnes), the aged Idumaean usurper feared a rival; Robertson notes that those who know “the story of Herod the Great in Josephus can well understand” his murderous suspicion. The Eastern Pseudo-Chrysostom adds the dynastic dread — that, “himself an Idumaean,” he feared “the kingdom should return to native princes.” Calvin observes that Herod “was not unacquainted with the predictions” of a coming King.

The fathers dwell on the meaning of a king of earth trembling at the King of heaven. Augustine: “as the Magi seek a Redeemer, so Herod fears a successor”; and “if his birth as an infant makes proud kings tremble, what will his tribunal as a Judge do?” Leo addresses the tyrant: “Thou art troubled, Herod, without cause; thy nature cannot contain Christ, nor is the Lord of the world content with the narrow bounds of thy dominion.” Gregory finds it fitting that “at the birth of a King of Heaven, a king of earth is troubled,” for “earthly greatness is confounded when heavenly greatness shows itself.” The witnesses thus find in the verse a study of guilty power and a parable of the world’s response to Christ. Herod is troubled, for the coming of the true King threatens the false; and “all Jerusalem with him” — whether from fear of Herod’s rage, or from dull worldliness — shares the unease, when it should have rejoiced. The witnesses note the tragedy that the birth which brought the angels’ song and the Magi’s adoration brought to the king and the city only trouble: so the light that is joy to the humble is terror to the proud, and the Saviour of the world is, from his cradle, a stumbling-stone to those who will not have him reign.

The witnesses observe that the trouble of Herod and his city stands as the first opposition the new-born King would meet. From the cradle to the cross, the world that loves its own power is uneasy at the reign of Christ. The verse thus opens the long conflict between the kingdom of heaven and the kingdoms of earth, and warns that the coming of the true King is no comfort to those who will not yield their thrones to him. Where the humble rejoice, the proud are troubled — and the trouble of Herod foreshadows the rage that would seek the Saviour’s life.
4:
“And when he had gathered all the chief priests and scribes of the people together, he demanded of them where Christ should be born.” The witnesses note Herod’s consultation of the religious authorities. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the assembly: Gill and Lightfoot take it as the Sanhedrin; Barnes and Wesley explain the “chief priests” as the high priest with his deputy and “the heads of the twenty-four courses” of the priesthood (cf. 1 Chronicles 24:4; Luke 1:5), and the “scribes” as the doctors and interpreters of the law. Robertson notes the prophetic present in Herod’s question — “where the Christ is born” — Matthew preserving “the very words of Herod.”

The Eastern Chrysostom marks the irony of the scene: the Jews, “as long as he was out of their sight,” rehearse “the testimonies of him with truth,” reciting the very prophecy that condemns their later unbelief. Calvin, for the Reformed, observes that “though deep silence prevailed respecting Christ in the hall of Herod,” the mention of a King at once awakens “predictions which formerly lay in oblivion,” and Herod “conjectures” the truth. The witnesses thus find in the verse a striking exposure of head-knowledge without heart. Herod, the godless tyrant, must send to the scribes to learn where the Christ should be born; and the scribes, the keepers of the oracles, can answer at once from the prophet — yet neither the king who asks nor the scholars who answer will go to seek the King so near. The witnesses note the lesson: that a man may know the Scriptures perfectly and yet not come to Christ; that Herod’s inquiry is not faith but fear, and the scribes’ learning not devotion but mere information. The very accuracy of their answer (to come in v.5) becomes a witness against them, who pointed the Gentiles to Bethlehem but would not go themselves.

The witnesses press the warning hidden in Herod’s council. To handle the Scriptures and yet to use them only to serve one’s own ends — as Herod sought the prophecy not to worship but to destroy — is the worst abuse of sacred knowledge. And the scribes, who could cite the place of the Messiah’s birth yet felt no stir to seek him, show how learning may leave the heart untouched. The verse thus searches every reader who knows the truth of Christ without coming to him, and exposes the peril of a religion that is all knowledge and no obedience.
5:
“And they said unto him, In Bethlehem of Judaea: for thus it is written by the prophet…” The witnesses note the prompt and confident answer of the scribes. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that they reply “without any hesitation” (Gill), it being “a generally received notion” and “settled by prophecy” (Barnes) — the place of Messiah’s birth being plainly written (Micah 5:2). The scribes name Bethlehem at once, citing the prophet as their authority.

The witnesses dwell on the contrast the verse sets up. The keepers of the law know exactly where the Christ should be born and say so readily; their knowledge is sound, their citation accurate. Yet, the witnesses note, this very readiness deepens their guilt: they can point the heathen Magi to Bethlehem but will not stir the six miles to go and see. The fathers find here the tragedy of a people who held the prophecies but missed their fulfilment — able to instruct others in the way to Christ while themselves standing still. The witnesses thus read the verse as a sober warning that orthodoxy of doctrine is not the same as devotion of heart. The scribes answer truly, and their truth serves the Magi and condemns themselves; for to know where Christ is and not to come to him is the very mark of an unbelief that no learning can excuse. The witnesses note that the answer also magnifies the providence of God, who used even the cold knowledge of unbelieving scribes to direct the seeking Gentiles to the manger — and, by Herod’s inquiry, to publish from the mouth of the Sanhedrin itself the testimony that the Christ was born in Bethlehem, as the prophet had foretold.

The witnesses draw the further lesson that God can make even the unbelief of men serve his truth. The scribes meant nothing by their answer but to satisfy the king; yet by it they bore public witness, before Herod and the Gentiles alike, that the Christ was born in Bethlehem. So the verse shows the providence that turns cold orthodoxy to account, publishing the testimony of Scripture from reluctant lips. The believer may take comfort that the purposes of God are not hindered by the dullness of those who hold the truth without loving it.
6:
“And thou Bethlehem, in the land of Juda, art not the least among the princes of Juda: for out of thee shall come a Governor, that shall rule my people Israel.” The witnesses examine the prophecy of Micah 5:2 as the scribes cite it. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the citation differs somewhat from both the Hebrew and the Greek of Micah — Matthew (or the scribes) giving the sense rather than the letter. The Latin Jerome marks that where the prophet says “Bethlehem Ephratah… though thou be little,” the meaning is that Bethlehem, “least” among the towns in size, is yet “not the least” in honour, since the Ruler comes from her. The Eastern Chrysostom presses the exactness: the prophet says not “he shall be in Bethlehem,” but “shall come out of Bethlehem,” showing “that he should be only born there” — against any application to Zerubbabel.

The witnesses dwell on the dignity the prophecy confers and the King it foretells. Bethlehem, the little town of David, is exalted above the great cities of Judah because from her comes the “Governor” who “shall rule my people Israel” — the long-promised Shepherd-King of David’s line. Gregory finds it fitting that the Bread of life should be born in Bethlehem, which means “house of bread.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the convergence of prophecy and event. The scribes, answering Herod, unwittingly proclaim the credentials of the very Child the Magi seek: born in Bethlehem, as Micah foretold, he is the Ruler of Israel, the Shepherd of God’s people. The witnesses note how God overrules the scene so that the testimony of Scripture is sounded aloud in Herod’s court — the place, the dignity, and the office of the Messiah all declared from the prophet — that none might say the Christ came unannounced. The humble town is honoured, the royal Shepherd is named, and the Gentiles are pointed to the cradle of the King, all by the word of a prophet spoken seven centuries before.

The witnesses add that the prophecy joins lowliness and majesty in a single breath, as the whole nativity does. The least of towns brings forth the Ruler of Israel; the manger holds the Shepherd-King. So the verse prepares the reader to find greatness in humility and the King in a cradle, and to honour the Bethlehem that the world overlooked. The Governor foretold by Micah is no earthly prince to rival Herod, but the everlasting Shepherd of God’s people, whose rule is gentleness and whose throne is grace.
7:
“Then Herod, when he had privily called the wise men, enquired of them diligently what time the star appeared.” The witnesses note the king’s secrecy and his hidden design. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the stealth: Robertson marks the word “privily” (lathra) — Herod “conceals” his real concern from the Sanhedrin, calling the Magi apart to learn “accurately” (akribōs) when the star appeared (Barnes, Ellicott). Calvin observes that “the tyrant did not dare to avow his fear,” lest he embolden the people who hated him, and so “pretends that this matter does not concern him.” The Latin Remigius reads the diligence as craft, for Herod “feared they would not return to him,” and wished to fix the time so as “to put the young Child to death.”

The fathers mark the hypocrisy beneath the inquiry. Pseudo-Chrysostom notes that, though the answer was “doubly authenticated” by the priests and the prophets, Herod “turned not to worship the King.” Gregory says plainly that “he feigns a wish of worshipping him only that he may discover him, and put him to death.” Pseudo-Augustine and Chrysostom add that the star had appeared “nearly two years before,” the journey of the Magi having taken time. The witnesses thus find in the verse the cunning of a guilty heart. Herod, masking murder under the show of devotion, works in secret — calling the Magi apart, probing for the exact time, that he may calculate the child’s age and strike with certainty. The witnesses note the contrast that runs through the chapter: the Magi seek the King openly, to worship; the king seeks him secretly, to slay. And they observe the providence overruling even Herod’s craft, for the very time he so carefully ascertained would, in his rage, become the measure of his cruelty (v.16), while the Child he sought to destroy was already under the keeping of God.

The witnesses note that Herod’s secrecy is itself a confession of his guilt. He dares not seek the King openly, as the Magi did, but must work in the dark, hiding his fear from his own council and his murderous intent under a show of piety. So the verse exposes the way of all who oppose Christ from a guilty heart: they cannot bear the light, but plot in secret. And it magnifies the folly of fighting against God, for all Herod’s careful inquiry only served, in the end, the purposes of the heaven he sought to thwart.
8:
“And he sent them to Bethlehem, and said, Go and search diligently for the young child; and when ye have found him, bring me word again, that I may come and worship him also.” The witnesses dwell on Herod’s consummate hypocrisy. The grammatical-historical commentators note that Bethlehem was “but a short six miles from Jerusalem” (Ellicott), and that Herod bids the Magi “search out exactly” (Robertson) and report, on the pretence that he too would worship. Clarke names it bluntly: “What exquisite hypocrisy was here! he only wished to find out the child that he might murder him.” Wesley wonders whether Herod even believed the child was born, since so suspicious a prince might otherwise have made “sure work at once.”

The witnesses dwell on the mask of devotion over a murderous intent. Herod uses the very language of the Magi — “that I may come and worship him also” — turning the words of true homage into a cover for slaughter. The witnesses find here the depth to which a guilty heart will sink, cloaking the design of murder in the speech of piety. Yet they note, too, the overruling hand of God. Herod’s plan depended on the Magi’s return; he could not himself find the child, and must trust the strangers to report. But the same providence that brought the Magi to Bethlehem would send them home another way (v.12), defeating the king’s scheme at its hinge. The witnesses thus read the verse as a study in the impotence of malice against the purposes of God: Herod lays his trap with cunning words, but the Child he seeks is guarded by heaven, and the very messengers he hoped to use will be warned against him. The hypocrite’s “that I may worship him also” is answered not by the Magi’s obedience to him, but by God’s protection of his Son.

The witnesses press the lesson that no cunning of man can outwit the providence of God. Herod laid his plan with a tyrant’s care, binding the Magi to report and cloaking his malice in the language of worship; yet the whole scheme hung on a return that God would forbid. The verse thus assures the believer that the rage of the mighty against Christ and his people is held in the hand of God, who turns their craft to nothing. The hypocrite’s smooth words deceive men, but they do not deceive heaven, nor bind the Child whom the Father guards.
9:
“When they had heard the king, they departed; and, lo, the star, which they saw in the east, went before them, till it came and stood over where the young child was.” The witnesses dwell on the reappearing of the star and its service. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the star, hidden while they were in Jerusalem, now reappears: Lightfoot supposes it had “shone in the very birth-night” and since disappeared; Robertson reads the imperfect “went before them” (proēgen), leading them not to the town, which they now knew, but “to the place where the child was.” Barnes takes it as “a luminous meteor… at no great distance from the ground,” which “stood over” the very house (Clarke, “over the head of the child”). The Latin Augustine reflects that the star, “to perform its due service to the Lord, advanced slowly, leading them… ministering to him, and not ruling his fate.”

The fathers read the star mystically. Ambrose says “the star is the way, and the way is Christ… the bright and morning star” — so that “where Herod is, the star is not seen; where Christ is, it is seen again.” Remigius makes the star the grace of God and Herod the devil, the soul that sins losing the grace, that repents finding it again. Calvin, for the Reformed, notes the “base sluggishness” of the Jews, not one offering to escort the foreigners to their own promised King. The witnesses thus find in the verse the renewed guidance of God to the seeking Magi. Having done with Herod and the dull scribes, they are led the last mile by the star, which returns to bring them to the very house. The witnesses note the tender providence: the same God who had drawn them from afar does not leave them to find the child unaided, but sends again the heavenly light to rest over the place, that the long journey of faith might end in the finding of the King. And they mark the silent reproach — that a star guided Gentiles to Christ while his own people would not stir to seek him.
10:
“When they saw the star, they rejoiced with exceeding great joy.” The witnesses dwell on the fulness of the Magi’s gladness. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the heaped-up language: Robertson notes the cognate accusative, “they rejoiced with great joy exceedingly,” and Barnes refers it to the success of their long search (cf. Psalm 67:4). They knew the star “by its appearance… to be the same” they had seen in their own country (Gill), and rejoiced that their hope was confirmed. The Latin Remigius observes that it was “not enough to say they rejoiced,” but “with exceeding great joy,” a joy answering to the greatness of what they had found.

The fathers find in their joy the gladness of faith. The Gloss notes that “he rejoices indeed who rejoices on God’s account, who is the true joy.” Pseudo-Chrysostom: they rejoiced “because their hopes were not falsified but confirmed, and the toil of so great travel had not been undertaken in vain.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the reward of a seeking faith. The Magi, who had left their distant land on the slender guidance of a star, who had endured a long and uncertain journey, and who had been left to ask their way among an indifferent people, are now met again by the heavenly sign, and their hearts overflow. The witnesses note that such is ever the joy of those who seek Christ and find him: a gladness “exceeding great,” out of all proportion to the world’s pleasures, springing from the certainty that the search is not in vain. The little band of Gentiles, drawing near to the house where the King lay, rejoice as men who have found the desire of their hearts — and their joy stands in bright contrast to the trouble of Herod and the dullness of Jerusalem, a foretaste of the joy with which the nations would one day come to the light of Christ.

The witnesses dwell on the contrast between this joy and the trouble of Jerusalem. The same coming of Christ that filled Herod and his city with dismay fills the seeking Gentiles with exceeding joy. So the verse teaches that the gospel is gladness or grief according to the heart that meets it: to the proud who will not yield, a trouble; to the humble who seek him, a joy unspeakable. The Magi’s overflowing gladness is the pattern of every soul that finds the Saviour, and a foretaste of the joy with which the nations would come to his light.
11:
“And when they were come into the house, they saw the young child with Mary his mother, and fell down, and worshipped him: and when they had opened their treasures, they presented unto him gifts; gold, and frankincense, and myrrh.” The witnesses dwell on the adoration and the threefold gift. The grammatical-historical commentators note the homage: they “fell down” in the Eastern manner of reverence (Barnes; cf. Daniel 3:7), and “opened their treasures” (thēsaurous, caskets, Robertson; Ellicott) to present their gifts, for “the people of the east never approach a king without a present” (Clarke). Calvin, for the Reformed, marvels at their faith, for the child was “in a meaner and more despised condition” than any king, yet they worshipped.

The fathers read the gifts as a confession of Christ’s person and work. Gregory and Augustine give the classic interpretation: “gold, as to a King; frankincense, as sacrifice to God; myrrh, as embalming the body of the dead” — the three gifts owning him King, God, and the One who would die for the sins of all. The Eastern Chrysostom turns the scene against the heretics: “let Marcion and Paul of Samosata blush, who will not see what the Magi saw” — Gentiles “adoring God in the flesh.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the climax of the Magi’s pilgrimage and a summary of the gospel. The wise men, kneeling before a peasant child in a humble house, render the worship due to God alone; and their gifts, beyond their own knowing, proclaim the mystery of his person — gold for his royalty, frankincense for his deity, myrrh for his death. The witnesses note that this Gentile adoration is the firstfruits of the nations’ homage, the kings of the earth bringing their treasures to Christ (cf. Psalm 72:10; Isaiah 60:6); and that true faith, like theirs, sees through the lowliness of the manger to the glory of the King, and falls down to worship.

The witnesses add that the worship of the Magi rebukes every notion that the lowliness of Christ excuses unbelief. They found no palace and no crown, only a child in a poor house with his mother; yet faith looked past the meanness to the majesty, and they fell down and adored. So the verse calls the reader to the same discernment — to see in the humble Christ the King and God he is, and to bring him not gold and spices but the homage of the heart. The Gentiles’ gifts, owning him King, God, and dying Saviour, are a confession the church has never ceased to make.
12:
“And being warned of God in a dream that they should not return to Herod, they departed into their own country another way.” The witnesses note the divine warning and the Magi’s obedient faith. The grammatical-historical commentators explain “warned of God” (chrēmatisthentes, Robertson — divinely instructed, as by an oracle) “in a dream,” to prevent their reporting to Herod, who would then have known where to send to slay the child (Barnes). The Latin Jerome notes that the warning came “not by an angel, but by the Lord himself,” to mark “the high merit” of Joseph and the Magi; the Gloss adds it was given by him who said, “I am the way” (John 14:6), now teaching them another way home.

The fathers admire the Magi’s simple faith and draw a moral. The Eastern Chrysostom: “see the faith of the Magi; they were not offended” nor questioned the need of flight, for “true faith asks not the why.” Pseudo-Chrysostom notes that, having only worshipped, “they go their way,” seeking no earthly advantage. Gregory finds a parable: “our country is Paradise, to which, after we have come to the knowledge of Christ, we are forbidden to return the way we came” — having left it by pride and disobedience, we return by another road, of humility and obedience. The witnesses thus find in the verse both the providence that guards the child and a pattern for the believer. God himself intervenes to thwart Herod, sending the Magi home another way, so that the murderous king is left waiting in vain. And in their changed route the fathers see the way of all who have found Christ: they do not go back as they came, but, turned by grace, take a new path — the soul that has met the Saviour returning to God not by the old way of sin but by the new way of faith and obedience.

The witnesses observe how the verse joins divine protection to human obedience. God warns, and the Magi obey at once, asking no questions and seeking no advantage; and by their simple faith the child is shielded from Herod’s reach. The verse thus commends the obedience that follows God’s word without argument, and assures the believer that the same providence which guarded the infant Christ watches over his people still, turning even the warnings of a dream to the defeat of their enemies and the keeping of his purpose.
13:
“And when they were departed, behold, the angel of the Lord appeareth to Joseph in a dream, saying, Arise, and take the young child and his mother, and flee into Egypt, and be thou there until I bring thee word: for Herod will seek the young child to destroy him.” The witnesses note the renewed angelic guidance and the flight from danger. The grammatical-historical commentators mark that, the Magi being gone, the angel again warns Joseph “in a dream,” the mode of revelation throughout these chapters; he is to “arise” at once, take mother and child, and flee to Egypt, the nearest land outside Herod’s reach, and remain until further word. The reason is given plainly: “Herod will seek the young child to destroy him.” The Latin Rabanus notes that Matthew omits the presentation in the temple (Luke 2:22), which preceded the flight.

The fathers dwell on the providence and the mystery of the flight. Remigius marks that the angel appears “always to Joseph in sleep,” a sign that those who “rest from worldly cares… deserve” such revelation. The witnesses ponder the wonder that the Son of God, the King before whom the Magi fell, must flee like a refugee from a petty tyrant — the Almighty, in his infancy, carried into exile to escape a murderer. They find in it both the reality of his true humanity, subject to danger and flight, and the depth of his humiliation, who “came unto his own” and was hunted from his cradle. Yet the witnesses note the overruling care of God: the angel’s timely warning, the appointed refuge in Egypt, the promise of further word, all show the Child kept safe by heaven amid the rage of Herod. The witnesses thus read the verse as a meeting of weakness and sovereignty — the helpless infant fleeing in his mother’s arms, yet guarded by the angel of the Lord and ordered by a providence that would bring him back in safety, that not Herod’s sword but the Father’s purpose should determine the days of the Saviour.

The witnesses draw comfort from the flight itself. That the Saviour was carried into Egypt to escape a murderer shows that he entered fully into the lot of the persecuted and the homeless, sharing the dangers of our fallen world from his infancy. Yet the verse also displays the unsleeping care of God, who would not let Herod’s sword touch the Child before his hour. So the believer learns that exile and flight are no sign of God’s absence, but may be the very path of his protection, and that the Lord who guarded his Son in Egypt can keep his own in every place of refuge.
14:
“When he arose, he took the young child and his mother by night, and departed into Egypt.” The witnesses note Joseph’s immediate, reverent obedience. The grammatical-historical commentators mark that, as soon as he awoke, he “did as he was commanded” (Gill), arising “by night” to make the journey at once. Ellicott observes the “significantly reverential” form of the words — “the young child and his mother,” not “his wife and child” — befitting the holy charge. Lightfoot notes that Egypt was at that time “replenished with Jews above measure,” a natural and accessible refuge, where the family would not be friendless.

The witnesses dwell on the promptness and the providence of the flight. Joseph does not wait for daylight or count the cost, but rises in the night and obeys, taking the child and his mother on the long road to a foreign land. The witnesses find in this the same ready faith they marked at his first obedience (Matthew 1:24): the word of God is enough, and he acts upon it without delay. They note, too, the tender providence that ordered Egypt as the place of refuge — near at hand, outside Herod’s reach, and full of Jews among whom the holy family might find shelter. The witnesses thus read the verse as a quiet picture of faith and care together. The guardian of the Saviour, warned by the angel, flees by night to save the child from the sword; and God, who sent the warning, has prepared the refuge. The King of the Jews, hunted from his cradle, is carried down into Egypt — the land of the old bondage now made a sanctuary — that the purpose of the Father, and not the malice of Herod, might govern the days of his Son.

The witnesses dwell on the wonder folded into so plain a verse. The eternal Son, before whom the Magi had bowed, is carried as a helpless infant through the night to a foreign land, sharing the lot of the hunted and the homeless. Yet in Joseph’s instant rising the reader sees the faith that asks no questions, and in the prepared refuge of Egypt the care of a God who orders all things for his Son. So the flight by night joins the deepest humiliation of Christ to the surest providence of the Father, and teaches the believer that the path of obedience, even into exile, is the path of safety.
15:
“And was there until the death of Herod: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken of the Lord by the prophet, saying, Out of Egypt have I called my son.” The witnesses note the brief sojourn and the prophecy it fulfilled. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the stay in Egypt was short, Herod dying soon after (Gill, Barnes), and that the citation is from Hosea 11:1, “Out of Egypt have I called my son.” They note the interpretive question: the words in Hosea refer directly to Israel’s deliverance from Egypt at the Exodus (Clarke, Calvin), so that Matthew applies them to Christ by way of type — Wesley explaining that in New Testament usage a scripture is “fulfilled” when “an event happens to which it may with great propriety be accommodated,” not always by direct prediction.

The witnesses dwell on the typology the Evangelist draws. Israel was God’s “son,” called out of Egypt to be his people; Christ is the true Son, who recapitulates and fulfils Israel’s history, himself called out of Egypt. The witnesses find in this a rich design: the Saviour, in his infancy, retraces the path of the nation — going down into Egypt and being called up out of it — so that what was said of the type, Israel, finds its deepest truth in the antitype, the Son of God. Calvin, for the Reformed, defends the application against those who would sever it from Hosea, holding that the prophet’s words point beyond the historical Exodus to the One in whom Israel’s sonship is fulfilled. The witnesses thus read the verse as another instance of Matthew’s great theme — that the life of Christ answers to the Scriptures at every turn. The flight into Egypt and the return are not bare history but the fulfilment of a pattern woven into Israel’s story; and the Child carried down and brought up again is shown to be the true Son whom God called, in whom the whole history of his people reaches its goal.

The witnesses add that the verse reveals the unity of the two Testaments in the person of Christ. What was spoken of the nation is fulfilled in the Son, who gathers Israel’s whole story into himself. The believer is thus taught to read the Old Testament with eyes opened to Christ, in whom its history and its hope converge. The sojourn in Egypt, brief as it was, becomes a thread in the great pattern of fulfilment, binding the infant Saviour to the ancient people and showing him to be the true Son whom God called out of bondage.
16:
“Then Herod, when he saw that he was mocked of the wise men, was exceeding wroth, and sent forth, and slew all the children that were in Bethlehem, and in all the coasts thereof, from two years old and under, according to the time which he had diligently enquired of the wise men.” The witnesses recount the massacre and its meaning. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that Herod, finding the Magi had not returned, believed himself “deceived” (not that they intended mockery, Barnes), and in his fury slew the male children of Bethlehem and its district “from two years old and under,” reckoning by “the time he had diligently enquired” (Robertson; cf. v.7). Ellicott grants that, though Josephus does not record it, the deed is wholly “in keeping with” Herod’s known character; Clarke cites the pagan Macrobius as an apparent allusion to it. The Eastern Chrysostom marks the folly: Herod “ought to have feared,” perceiving he attempted “impossible things,” but his insensate rage was not restrained.

The fathers find in the slain infants the first martyrs for Christ. Augustine says that, “while he thus persecutes Christ, he furnished an army of martyrs clothed in white robes, of the same age as the Lord”; Bede reads their death as figuring “the precious death of all Christ’s martyrs,” and their infancy as teaching that “by the merit of humility alone can we come to the glory of martyrdom.” The witnesses thus find in the verse the dark fruit of Herod’s malice and the providence that overruled it. The tyrant, cheated of his prey, vents his rage on the innocents of Bethlehem — a deed of monstrous cruelty, yet powerless to touch the Child, already safe in Egypt. The witnesses note the bitter irony: Herod murders many to be sure of one, and misses the one; and the babes he slew, dying in the place of Christ and for his sake, are honoured by the church as the holy innocents, the first to shed their blood for the King they could not yet know. The malice of man rages, but the purpose of God stands, and even this horror is woven into the story of redemption.
17:
“Then was fulfilled that which was spoken by Jeremy the prophet, saying…” The witnesses note the Evangelist’s citation of prophecy over the massacre. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that the quotation (to come in v.18) is from Jeremiah 31:15, and that Jerome marks it as following “neither the Hebrew nor the LXX,” a sign that the Evangelists gave the sense, not a fixed translation. Barnes and Wesley explain “fulfilled” in the accommodated sense — the words “aptly express the event,” the sorrow of Jeremiah’s day finding a deeper echo in the weeping of Bethlehem. The Eastern Chrysostom notes the Evangelist’s pastoral aim: having “filled the reader with horror” at the massacre, he now “soothes his feelings,” showing the deed was foreseen and within the providence of God.

The fathers find comfort in the prophecy beyond the grief. Hilary insists the slain “could not be that they were not,” for “by glorious martyrdom they were advanced to eternal life”; the consolation withheld is the false comfort of supposing them lost, when in truth they live. Rabanus and the Gloss read the refusal of comfort as the church transferring “all her hope and comfort to the life to come,” unwilling to wish the departed saints back to the struggles of earth. The witnesses thus find in the verse the Evangelist binding even this atrocity to the word of God. The murder of the innocents was no accident outside the divine knowledge but the fulfilment of a prophet’s lament, so that the reader, appalled, may yet see the hand of God over all. The witnesses note how Matthew’s constant appeal to fulfilled Scripture steadies faith in the darkest scene: the horror of Bethlehem, like the flight to Egypt and the birth at Bethlehem, was foreseen and foretold, and the God who permitted the sorrow had already spoken of it, gathering even the tears of the mothers into the story of his Son.

The witnesses note how Matthew’s method steadies the trembling reader. By tracing even the massacre to the word of a prophet, he shows that the darkest events of the Saviour’s coming were within the foreknowledge and permission of God. This does not lessen the horror, but it forbids despair: nothing befell the holy family or the innocents of Bethlehem outside the divine purpose. The verse thus teaches the believer to look for the hand of God even in the most grievous providences, and to find, in the fulfilment of Scripture, a ground of faith amid sorrow.
18:
“In Rama was there a voice heard, lamentation, and weeping, and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children, and would not be comforted, because they are not.” The witnesses unfold the prophecy of Rachel’s lament. The grammatical-historical commentators explain its original setting: Clarke and Calvin note that the words in Jeremiah 31:15 described “the captivity of the ten tribes,” or the destruction of Benjamin in Jeremiah’s day, Rachel — the mother of Benjamin, buried near Bethlehem (Genesis 35:19) — being “beautifully represented” as risen from her grave to weep over her descendants. Ellicott marks this as another instance of Matthew’s applying to his own time a passage that “had a direct bearing” on an earlier event, the ancient sorrow aptly figuring the new.

The witnesses dwell on the fitness and the comfort of the image. Rachel, the mother of Israel, weeps over the children of Bethlehem as she had wept over the exiles of old — “would not be comforted, because they are not.” The fathers, however, find in the very prophecy a hidden hope: the children “are not” on earth, yet, Hilary and Rabanus insist, they live in glory, so that Rachel’s refusal of comfort looks not to despair but to the resurrection. The witnesses thus read the verse as the voice of a mother’s grief taken up into Scripture. The slaughter of the innocents is no meaningless cruelty but is given a place in the prophetic word, the weeping of Bethlehem joined to the weeping of Rachel over all Israel’s losses. And the witnesses note the deeper consolation the Evangelist points toward: the very chapter of Jeremiah that records Rachel’s tears goes on to promise that “thy children shall come again to their own border” (Jeremiah 31:17) — so that the lament over the innocents is set within a prophecy of hope, the sorrow of the mothers answered, in the purpose of God, by the coming of the very Child whom Herod sought to slay, the Saviour who turns mourning into joy.

The witnesses press the tenderness with which Scripture honours a mother’s grief. The weeping of Bethlehem is not passed over but gathered into the prophetic word, Rachel herself made the voice of every bereaved mother. Yet the lament is set within a chapter of hope, so that the sorrow points beyond itself to the comfort God would bring. The verse thus assures the mourner that his tears are seen and recorded by God, and that the grief which seems beyond comfort is held within a purpose of mercy that will, in the end, wipe every tear away.
19:
“But when Herod was dead, behold, an angel of the Lord appeareth in a dream to Joseph in Egypt.” The witnesses note the death of the tyrant and the renewed guidance to Joseph. The grammatical-historical commentators mark that Herod died soon after the massacre (Gill); Eusebius and Josephus record his dreadful end, which the fathers read as “the Divine vengeance” hastening his death for his crimes. Jerome carefully distinguishes this Herod (the Great) from the later Herods, lest readers confuse him with the Herod of the Passion. Once the persecutor is dead, the angel again appears to Joseph “in a dream” — the constant mode of these revelations — to call the family home.

The fathers and Reformers draw out the providence and the pattern. Calvin, for the Reformed, marks “the perseverance of Joseph’s faith,” who “kept his feet firm in Egypt till he was recalled… by a command of God,” neither presuming to return on his own nor lingering once called. Henry, for the Puritan expositors, notes that “Egypt may serve to sojourn in, or take shelter in, for a while, but not to abide in,” for Christ was sent to the lost sheep of Israel. Bede reads the whole — the slaughter, Herod’s death, and the return — as a figure that “all the persecutors” of the church shall perish, and the people of God be brought home. The witnesses thus find in the verse the turning of the tide. The malice that drove the Saviour into exile is spent; the persecutor is dead by the judgment of God; and the same angel who warned of Herod’s rage now brings word of his death, that the Child may be brought back. The witnesses note the lesson for the church: that no enemy of Christ endures forever, but God outlives them all, and in his time recalls his people from their place of refuge to the work he has for them.

The witnesses draw the comfort that no persecutor of Christ endures. Herod, who filled Bethlehem with blood to destroy one Child, is himself struck down, while the Child he sought lives on under the keeping of God. So the verse declares the certain end of all who set themselves against the Lord and his Anointed: they pass, and he remains. The believer learns to wait upon God in the days of the oppressor, assured that the rage of the mighty is brief, and that the Lord outlives every enemy and recalls his people in his time.
20:
“Saying, Arise, and take the young child and his mother, and go into the land of Israel: for they are dead which sought the young child’s life.” The witnesses note the command to return and the plural “they are dead.” The grammatical-historical commentators discuss the plural: Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott observe that, though Herod alone had been named, the phrase may use the plural for the singular, or may include his son Antipater (executed just before Herod) and others “implicated in the scheme.” Robertson reads it as “a general statement of a particular fact,” after the common idiom “they say.” The command echoes the words of the angel at the flight (v.13), now reversed: take the child and his mother, and go back, for the danger is past.

The witnesses dwell on the tender exactness of the divine care. The angel’s words — “the young child and his mother” — keep the same reverent form as before, the holy charge ever first in view. And the ground given is the removal of the threat: “they are dead which sought the young child’s life.” The witnesses note the echo of the LORD’s word to Moses, “all the men are dead which sought thy life” (Exodus 4:19), so that the return of the infant Saviour from Egypt is framed in the very language of Moses’ return — another touch by which Christ retraces and fulfils the history of Israel and its deliverer. The witnesses thus find in the verse the gentle providence that brings the family home. The God who sent them into exile to escape the sword now recalls them, the persecutors dead, the way clear; and the Child whom Herod could not reach is brought back in safety to the land of promise. The witnesses note the assurance this gives the believer — that the same hand which leads into the place of refuge leads out again in its time, and that no enemy of God’s purpose outlasts the patience of God.

The witnesses observe how the verse mirrors the deliverance of Moses, so that the infant Saviour is shown to walk the path of Israel’s deliverer. As Moses was told to return when those who sought his life were dead, so the Christ is brought back from Egypt when his persecutors are gone. The believer is thus taught to see in the infancy of Jesus the gathering up of the whole story of redemption, and to trust the God who, having led into the place of refuge, knows the hour to lead safely home.
21:
“And he arose, and took the young child and his mother, and came into the land of Israel.” The witnesses note Joseph’s exact and prompt obedience once more. The grammatical-historical commentators observe that “Joseph was not disobedient to the angelic warning” (Gloss), but “exactly conformed in every circumstance” (Gill) — arising, taking the child and his mother, and returning to the land of Israel, as he was bidden. The angel had named the land but “not fixed the particular place” (Gloss), leaving Joseph to be further guided, as the next verse shows.

The fathers read the return figuratively. Hilary takes Joseph as a figure of the apostles, to whom Christ is “entrusted to be borne about,” bringing him back to Israel when the persecutor is dead; Rabanus applies it to “the last times of the Jewish Church,” when many shall turn to Christ. The witnesses dwell on the steady obedience the verse records. For the third time in these chapters (cf. Matthew 1:24; 2:14), Joseph hears the word of God and at once does it, without question or delay — fleeing when told to flee, returning when told to return, ever the faithful guardian of the Saviour and his mother. The witnesses commend in him the pattern of a life ordered wholly by the will of God, ready at every turn to obey. They note, too, the providence that left the precise destination open: Joseph returns to the land, but waits upon further guidance for the place, learning that the obedient soul is led step by step, told enough to act but kept dependent on God for the next direction. The Child is brought home to Israel; and the manner of his settling there, in the despised Nazareth, will be the last of the wonders by which Matthew shows the infancy of Christ fulfilling the Scriptures.

The witnesses commend in Joseph a faith that neither runs ahead of God nor lags behind him. Told to return, he returns at once; not told the precise place, he waits for further word. So the verse models the obedience that acts on what is known and trusts God for what is not yet shown. The guardian of the Saviour is led, as all the faithful are led, one step at a time, and proves that the surest way is simply to do, without delay or addition, the thing that God commands.
22:
“But when he heard that Archelaus did reign in Judaea in the room of his father Herod, he was afraid to go thither: notwithstanding, being warned of God in a dream, he turned aside into the parts of Galilee.” The witnesses note Joseph’s fear and the further divine direction. The grammatical-historical commentators explain that Archelaus, Herod’s son, had inherited “a cruel and tyrannical disposition similar to his father” (Barnes) — at one Passover slaying three thousand of the people — so that Joseph, learning he reigned in Judaea, “was afraid to go thither.” Robertson and Ellicott note the divided kingdom: Archelaus held Judaea, while Galilee fell to the milder Antipas. Again “warned of God in a dream,” Joseph turns aside to Galilee.

The witnesses dwell on the union of prudence and guidance. Joseph’s natural fear of Archelaus was not faithlessness but a reasonable caution, which God did not rebuke but confirmed and directed by a fresh warning. The witnesses find here a model of how God leads his people: not setting aside their judgment, but working through it, prompting the fear and then guiding the step. Joseph reasons rightly that Judaea is unsafe, and God, by a dream, settles the matter and sends him to Galilee. The witnesses thus read the verse as a quiet lesson in providence and means. The same God who could have protected the child in Judaea chooses instead to lead him to Galilee, using Joseph’s fear and a dream together to bring the family to the place appointed. They note that this further turn was not idle but served the last of Matthew’s fulfilments: that the Saviour should be reared not in the royal Judaea but in despised Galilee, in the town of Nazareth, that he might be called a Nazarene. The fear of Archelaus, overruled by God, becomes the means by which the prophecy of Christ’s lowly home is brought to pass.

The witnesses note how God honours the cautious judgment of his servant. Joseph’s fear of Archelaus was no failure of faith but a prudence that God confirmed and guided, sending him by a dream to Galilee. So the verse teaches that providence ordinarily works through, not against, the reasonable fears and choices of God’s people, prompting and directing them to the place appointed. The believer may bring his honest fears to God, assured that the Lord will use even them to lead him in the right way.
23:
“And he came and dwelt in a city called Nazareth: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophets, He shall be called a Nazarene.” The witnesses note the settling in Nazareth and the difficult prophecy. The grammatical-historical commentators wrestle with the citation, for no single Old Testament text says “He shall be called a Nazarene.” Several explanations are offered: that it refers to Netzer, the “Branch” of Isaiah 11:1 (“a Branch shall grow out of his roots”), Nazareth and Netzer sharing a root; that it alludes to the Nazarite of the Law (Numbers 6:2), Christ being holy and separated to God; or that “Nazarene” stands for one despised and lowly, since Nazareth was held in contempt (John 1:46, “Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?”). Jerome marks that Matthew writes “the prophets,” not “the prophet,” using the plural because he gives “not the words of any one passage” but the general sense of many.

The fathers note the same point. Jerome and Pseudo-Chrysostom observe that Matthew appeals to the prophets collectively, gathering their witness that the Messiah should be despised and rejected (cf. Isaiah 53:3; Psalm 22:6). The witnesses thus find in the verse the fitting close of the infancy narrative. The King born in Bethlehem, worshipped by the Magi, hunted by Herod, and called out of Egypt, comes at last to dwell in an obscure and scorned town of Galilee — and even this lowliness fulfils the Scriptures. The witnesses read “He shall be called a Nazarene” as Matthew’s summary of the prophetic testimony to a Messiah of humble and despised estate: whether by the Branch of Jesse, the holy Nazarite, or the reproach of Nazareth, the prophets had foretold a Christ who would be lowly and rejected, and so it came to pass. The witnesses note how fittingly the chapter ends — the genealogy of kings, the virgin birth, the star and the gifts, all coming to rest in a carpenter’s home in Nazareth, that the Saviour might be at once the Son of David and the despised Nazarene, fulfilling every word that was written of him.
